The term "White Revolution" refers to two distinct movements that took place in different parts of the world. 

The first White Revolution, also known as the Shah and People Revolution, was a series of reforms launched by Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, the Shah of Iran, in 1963. The revolution aimed to modernize the Imperial State of Iran through various reforms, including land reform, expansion of the road, rail, and air network, dam and irrigation projects, disease eradication, industrial growth, and promotion of women's rights. The revolution ended with the overthrow of the Shah in 1979.

On the other hand, the White Revolution in India, also known as Operation Flood, was a rural development program launched in the 1970s. The program's primary goal was to increase milk production and improve the livelihoods of rural communities through dairy development. The program was successful in achieving its objectives and had a significant impact on India's dairy industry, transforming the country into one of the world's largest milk producers.

While the two movements share the same name, they have distinct differences in terms of their objectives, scope, and outcomes. The Iranian White Revolution was a broader movement that aimed to modernize the entire country, whereas the Indian White Revolution was a sector-specific program focused on dairy development. Despite these differences, both movements have left a lasting legacy in their respective countries, with the Indian White Revolution continuing to contribute to the country's economic development and the Iranian White Revolution remaining an important part of the country's history.
